flashcards.back: The process of finding the derivative of a function with respect to a single variable, measuring the instantaneous rate of change.
The process of finding the derivative of a function of multiple variables with respect to one variable while holding others constant.
The derivative of a function f(x,y) with respect to x, treating y as constant. Denoted as ∂f/∂x or fₓ.

flashcards.front: State the Chain Rule for Partial Derivatives
flashcards.back: If z = f(x,y) where x = g(t), y = h(t), then dz/dt = (∂z/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂z/∂y)(dy/dt).
The derivative of a function with respect to an independent variable considering all indirect dependencies. For z = f(x,y), dz/dt = ∂z/∂x(dx/dt) + ∂z/∂y(dy/dt).
A function f(x,y) is homogeneous of degree n if f(λx, λy) = λⁿf(x,y) for any scalar λ.

flashcards.front: State Euler's Theorem for Homogeneous Functions
flashcards.back: If f(x,y) is homogeneous of degree n, then x(∂f/∂x) + y(∂f/∂y) = nf(x,y).
The determinant of the matrix of first-order partial derivatives, denoted as J = ∂(u,v)/∂(x,y) = |∂u/∂x ∂u/∂y; ∂v/∂x ∂v/∂y|.

flashcards.front: Define Maxima and Minima in Multivariable Functions
flashcards.back: A point (a,b) is a maximum if f(a,b) ≥ f(x,y) for all nearby points; minimum if f(a,b) ≤ f(x,y). Requires ∂f/∂x = ∂f/∂y = 0.
A square matrix of second-order partial derivatives: H = [∂²f/∂x² ∂²f/∂x∂y; ∂²f/∂y∂x ∂²f/∂y²]. Used to test concavity.

flashcards.front: Define Taylor's Series for Two Variables
flashcards.back: f(x+h, y+k) = f(x,y) + h∂f/∂x + k∂f/∂y + (h²/2!)∂²f/∂x² + ... Used for function approximation.
For M(x,y)dx + N(x,y)dy to be exact, ∂M/∂y must equal ∂N/∂x. Then the differential represents the total differential of some function.

flashcards.front: Define Gradient of a Scalar Function
flashcards.back: ∇f = (∂f/∂x)i + (∂f/∂y)j + (∂f/∂z)k. A vector pointing in the direction of maximum increase.
Finding dy/dx when y is defined implicitly as f(x,y)=0. Differentiate both sides with respect to x, treating y as function of x.
The rate of change of f in the direction of a unit vector u. Dᵤf = ∇f · u = fₓa + fᵧb where u = (a,b).
